description: Evaluates whether the researcher_worker correctly routes sub-dispatch tasks within its chaining budget, handles tasks directly when sub-dispatch is unnecessary, and respects worker-layer lane boundaries. Limited delegation: only correct_delegation (3.1.x) sub-metrics are evaluated; handoff_quality (3.2.x) and pipeline_adherence (3.3.x) are not applicable to workers.

specialist_routing.description: When the researcher encounters a genuinely orthogonal sub-question during investigation that warrants sub-dispatch within the chaining budget, the sub-worker is dispatched to the correct archetype based on the sub-question's domain. Research sub-questions go to researcher_worker. The researcher does not mis-route sub-questions to archetypes that do not match the sub-task domain.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: The researcher correctly identifies when a sub-question belongs to a different domain and dispatches it to the matching worker archetype. Routing decisions are traceable in the agent's reasoning trace showing domain classification before routing.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: In agent logs, the Task tool call for sub-dispatch specifies the correct worker archetype matching the sub-question domain. Rejected or mis-routed sub-questions appear as failed sub-tasks or sub-dispatches to incorrect archetypes.

direct_handling.description: Researcher handles the dispatched investigation directly without sub-dispatching when the task is answerable within its own context. Sub-dispatch is reserved for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions. The researcher does not over-delegate trivial sub-questions. Default is no sub-dispatch.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: The researcher completes the majority of research tasks within its own context without spawning sub-workers, even when chaining budget is available. Sub-dispatch only occurs for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the researcher completing investigation steps within its own context. When chaining budget is zero, no Task tool calls for sub-dispatch appear. Over-delegation appears as excessive Task tool calls.

lane_boundary_respect.description: Researcher respects the worker-layer lane boundary: sub-dispatches go only to other workers, never to leads or to the CEO. The researcher does not bypass the lead that dispatched it and does not attempt to dispatch lead-level agents.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All sub-dispatches target worker-layer agents and never lead-layer agents. When the researcher cannot resolve an issue, it escalates to its dispatching lead via the return protocol, not attempt to dispatch around the hierarchy.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: In agent logs, all Task tool calls for sub-dispatch target worker archetypes, never lead archetypes. Attempts to dispatch to leads appear as rejected Task tool calls or archetype mismatch errors.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: an active research brief in research_brief.md (which outlines the investigation scope for this run, including the specific research question, success criteria, and expected deliverables), a previous partial finding in preliminary_notes.md (containing interim notes from an earlier session on this investigation), and a lead dispatch summary in dispatch_context.json that includes your chaining budget allocation and any special instructions from your lead. You should read these files using the Read tool before proceeding with any investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following explicit mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which garbage collection pause times affect tail latency in JVM-based services. Focus on: (1) how GC safepoints interact with thread scheduling to create pause time events, (2) the statistical distribution of pause times and why the tail is relevant for SLA compliance (p99, p999), (3) empirical benchmarks from production environments showing pause time percentiles. This investigation may surface orthogonal sub-questions requiring specialized systems research. Your chaining budget is available for appropriate sub-dispatches.' The brief emphasizes that you should explore both the theoretical mechanism connecting GC pause times to latency tail behavior and the empirical evidence from production environments demonstrating this phenomenon. Your lead has specifically noted that understanding the mechanism requires tracing the causal chain from GC algorithm behavior through JVM thread scheduling to application-level latency spikes.

Your chaining budget for this investigation is: max depth 1, max fan-out 2. This means you may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth beyond yourself, and you may spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers.

Before beginning your investigation, read the files listed above. You should then plan your investigation approach, considering whether any sub-questions surfacing during your investigation would benefit from sub-dispatching to other agents within your chaining budget. Your output should be a comprehensive mechanistic explanation of the GC-pause-to-tail-latency causal chain, supported by empirical evidence and statistical analysis. The target audience is a senior systems engineer who understands JVM internals at a high level but needs the specific causal mechanism and quantitative evidence to act on the findings. Your output should include: a clear causal chain diagram (described in text), statistical distributions of pause times and their tail behavior, empirical benchmarks from production environments, and actionable implications for SLA compliance. You should trace the full causal chain from GC algorithm behavior (e.g., G1GC stop-the-world pauses) through JVM thread scheduling (safepoint mechanisms, thread resume latency) to application-level latency spikes (how a 50ms pause creates a p99 spike in a high-concurrency service).

Your investigation approach should follow a structured three-part analysis. First, establish the theoretical foundation: explain how garbage collection safepoints work in the JVM — when the GC decides to collect, all application threads must reach a safepoint before the GC can proceed, threads are brought to safepoints by having them check a safepoint flag at method entry points and loop back edges. The time it takes to bring all threads to safepoints is itself a component of pause time. Second, analyze the statistical distribution of pause times: GC pauses are not normally distributed — they follow heavy-tailed distributions where the mean is not representative. For SLA compliance at p99 and p999, you need to characterize the tail behavior, not just the mean. Third, present empirical benchmarks from production environments: real-world data showing pause time percentiles in production JVM services, including data from systems similar to yours. Your analysis should connect these three parts into a unified causal chain that explains exactly how GC behavior produces tail latency violations. Cite specific sources for empirical data.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing: the researcher should sub-dispatch the OS-level sub-question to another researcher_worker rather than attempting to absorb systems-level investigation outside its immediate dispatch scope. The sub-question is genuinely orthogonal (OS-level memory management vs application-level GC mechanism) and warrants delegation to a specialized researcher rather than being absorbed or mis-routed to a non-research archetype.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: the lead's research brief in dispatch_brief.md which contains detailed instructions, a sources directory at sources/ containing three primary source documents you need to read, and a cross_check_template.md outlining the format for your final analysis. You should use the Read tool to examine these files before proceeding with any investigation work.

The dispatch_brief.md contains the following explicit mandate: 'Research the principle-vs-tactic classification of the "strangler fig" migration pattern. This is a classification exercise requiring careful source analysis. You must: (1) read all three primary sources in full, (2) cross-check them for consistency in how they categorize the pattern, (3) produce a final classification verdict with supporting evidence from each source, (4) note any disagreements between sources and propose a resolution methodology. Your chaining budget is set to zero for this investigation. You are the terminal point. No sub-dispatches are permitted. Complete all work directly.' The brief emphasizes that this is a classification exercise where you must determine whether the strangler fig pattern is best classified as a principle (a fundamental guiding concept) or a tactic (a specific implementation technique), and that your verdict must be supported by explicit evidence from each source.

The sources directory contains three primary sources that you must read in full: sources/fowler_2003.md (Martin Fowler's original strangler fig article, the seminal work that established the pattern name and core concept, first published in 2003 and widely cited in both academic and industry contexts), sources/academic_migration.md (a peer-reviewed academic treatment of migration patterns that classifies the strangler fig among other patterns and provides theoretical grounding for the classification), and sources/industry_reference.md (an industry guide that provides practical context for how the pattern is used and classified in real-world migrations).

Your task is a classification exercise with zero chaining budget — you must complete all analysis directly. Read all three sources completely before forming any classification judgment. Cross-check them for consistency: do they agree on what the strangler fig is? Do they classify it the same way? Where they disagree, trace the disagreement to the root: is it a definitional disagreement (different definitions of principle vs tactic), a framing disagreement (same concept framed differently), or an empirical disagreement (different observations leading to different classifications)? Produce a final verdict with explicit evidence from each source, not just assertion. The output format should follow the cross_check_template.md structure.

Your approach to this classification exercise should be structured and methodical. Begin by reading all three sources in their entirety before forming any classification judgment. As you read each source, document: what definition of "principle" does this source use? What definition of "tactic"? How does this source characterize the strangler fig — as a guiding architectural philosophy or as a specific implementation technique? What evidence does the source provide for its classification? After reading all three, identify points of agreement and disagreement. Where sources agree, the classification is likely clearer. Where sources disagree, analyze whether the disagreement stems from different definitions, different framing, or genuinely different conclusions. Your final verdict should be explicit: is the strangler fig a principle or a tactic? Provide at least three specific pieces of evidence from the sources supporting your verdict. If the classification genuinely depends on definitions, state which definition you are using and why. Do not conclude that the classification is "it depends" without explaining what it depends on and how to resolve it.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling with zero chaining budget: the researcher must complete all investigation work directly without sub-dispatch, including source reading and cross-checking. The zero depth and zero fan-out constraints are explicit and binding. The researcher should not attempt to work around these constraints or treat them as advisory rather than mandatory.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research mandate in research_mandate.md from your lead specifying the investigation scope, a workspace state file in workspace_state.json showing what evidence has already been gathered, and a directory at evidence/ containing partial findings from your investigation so far. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding with any additional investigation work.

The research_mandate.md contains the following explicit instruction: 'Investigate whether QUIC provides measurable improvements over TCP for mobile network conditions. This investigation must cover three independent dimensions: (1) Protocol mechanism analysis how QUIC's stream multiplexing and 0-RTT resumption differ from TCP's congestion control approach, (2) Mobile network characteristics the specific properties of mobile networks (latency variability, packet loss patterns, handover behavior) that affect protocol performance, (3) Real-world benchmark data empirical evidence from production deployments and academic studies showing actual performance deltas. Each dimension should be treated as a distinct investigation thread with its own evidence base and analysis methodology. You should produce a unified analysis that shows how the three dimensions interrelate.'

The workspace_state.json shows you have already completed preliminary investigation on dimension 1 (protocol mechanism). Your partial notes in evidence/protocol_analysis.md show initial findings on QUIC vs TCP handshake differences, including the 0-RTT advantage and stream multiplexing benefits. You have documented how QUIC reduces connection establishment latency and how stream multiplexing allows independent stream recovery without head-of-line blocking.

Your task now is to build on your existing findings and complete the remaining two dimensions. For dimension 2 (mobile network characteristics), investigate how mobile networks differ from wired networks: latency variability (why mobile RTT fluctuates more), packet loss patterns (why mobile networks have different loss characteristics), and handover behavior (what happens to connections during cell tower transitions). For dimension 3 (real-world benchmarks), gather empirical evidence from production deployments and academic studies that quantify the actual performance delta between QUIC and TCP under mobile conditions. Your final output should unify all three dimensions into a coherent answer to the core question: does QUIC provide measurable improvements over TCP for mobile network conditions, and if so, through what mechanism?

Your investigation should follow a structured three-dimension approach. For dimension 1 (protocol mechanism analysis), you have already established that QUIC's stream multiplexing eliminates head-of-line blocking and that 0-RTT resumption reduces connection establishment latency. Build on this foundation by analyzing the specific QUIC protocol mechanisms: how does QUIC's connection ID differ from TCP's 4-tuple connection identification? How does this affect mobile network scenarios where the client IP changes during handover? How does QUIC's connection migration work and why does it matter for mobile networks? For dimension 2 (mobile network characteristics), investigate the specific properties that make mobile networks challenging: variable latency (why does mobile RTT fluctuate more than wired?), packet loss patterns (why does mobile have different loss characteristics than WiFi or wired?), and handover behavior (what happens to a QUIC connection when a phone moves between cell towers — does the connection survive?). For dimension 3 (real-world benchmark data), find empirical studies and production deployment reports that quantify the actual performance delta. What does the data show about QUIC vs TCP performance on mobile networks specifically? Be skeptical of benchmarks that compare QUIC and TCP in optimal lab conditions — focus on mobile-specific conditions. Your unified analysis should show how the three dimensions interact: how do mobile network characteristics amplify or diminish QUIC's protocol mechanism advantages?
prompts.rationale: Tests whether the researcher handles the three dimensions directly (preferred default) or correctly sub-dispatches only if the sub-questions are genuinely orthogonal and cannot be efficiently answered within its own context. The default should be direct handling; sub-dispatch should only occur for genuinely orthogonal sub-questions. The three dimensions may be related enough that the researcher should handle them directly rather than fragmenting the investigation across sub-workers.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a findings document in findings.md where you have been recording your investigation progress, and a directory at postgresql_src/ containing excerpts from the PostgreSQL source code relevant to B-tree index implementation. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding with any additional investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the causal mechanism behind database index bloat in PostgreSQL. Understanding index bloat requires understanding both the operational context (how indexes become bloated through delete/update patterns) and the implementation context (how B-tree structures in PostgreSQL handle page splits and garbage collection). Your investigation should produce a causal chain: operational patterns to implementation behavior to bloat manifestation. You should trace the full causal path from application-level operations through database engine behavior to the physical manifestation of bloat in index pages.'

Your findings.md shows you have already established the operational context. Through analysis of delete/update patterns and their effect on index page utilization, you have documented how UPDATE operations that migrate rows create dead index entries (the old index pointer remains after the row moves), and how DELETE operations leave tombstone entries (the index entry remains after the row is deleted). You have traced the operational causal chain up to the implementation boundary: the bloat originates from these dead entries but the mechanism by which they accumulate in B-tree pages requires implementation-level analysis.

The postgresql_src/ directory contains the implementation files you need to analyze: postgresql_src/btree_split.c (implementation of B-tree page split logic showing how dead entries are handled during page splits).

Your task now is to cross the implementation boundary. Read the btree_split.c source code and trace the mechanism: when a page splits, what happens to dead entries? Are they carried forward into new pages? Are they cleaned up? How does the page split algorithm interact with the vacuum mechanism? Your final output should complete the causal chain from operational patterns through B-tree implementation behavior to the physical manifestation of bloat in index pages. Produce a clear causal chain diagram (described in text) showing exactly how operational patterns lead to bloat through specific implementation mechanisms.

Your investigation should trace the complete causal chain from application-level operations to physical bloat manifestation. Start from the operational patterns you have already documented: UPDATE operations that migrate rows create dead index entries (the old index pointer is left pointing to the old row location, and when the row migrates, the old entry becomes orphaned but remains in the index), and DELETE operations that remove rows leave tombstone entries (the index entry remains after the row is deleted, serving no purpose but taking up space). Now cross into the implementation layer: read the btree_split.c source code and trace exactly what happens during a page split. When an index page becomes full and needs to split, the database must divide the entries between the old page and the new page. The key question for bloat: what happens to dead entries and tombstones during this split? Do they get carried forward into the new pages? Are they cleaned up? If they are carried forward, bloat accumulates over multiple splits and never gets cleaned up unless vacuum runs. Investigate the interaction between page splits and the vacuum mechanism: when vacuum runs, does it reclaim pages that are mostly dead entries? How does the fillfactor setting affect page split frequency and bloat accumulation? Your final output should make the causal chain explicit and quantitative: given a specific workload pattern (e.g., 10% updates, 5% deletes), how much bloat accumulates per week, and what is the impact on query performance?
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the researcher should not dispatch to backend_developer_worker for source code reading since the researcher has read access and code reading is within research scope. The B-tree implementation details are legitimate research material. If the sub-question is truly about code analysis beyond research scope, the researcher escalates to its lead rather than attempting to dispatch a lead-level agent. The key insight is that having read access to source code means the researcher can handle this investigation directly.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in brief.md from your lead, a sub-task record in sub_task_1.json documenting a sub-investigation you previously dispatched, and a sub_task_1_result.json containing the result from your sub-worker. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which consistent hashing distributes load across nodes. This investigation should cover: (1) the algorithm mechanics how consistent hashing maps keys to nodes using a hash ring, (2) the hash ring data structure how virtual nodes create uniform distribution, (3) the virtual node mapping strategy how multiple virtual nodes per physical node improve load balancing. Your investigation should produce a mechanistic explanation of how the algorithm achieves uniform load distribution, with supporting mathematical analysis.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 2. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers. You have not yet used your full fan-out budget.

During your investigation, you dispatched a sub-task to a sub-worker (sub_task_1) using one of your two available fan-out slots. The sub_task_1.json record shows: you asked the sub-worker to 'investigate the mathematical properties of consistent hashing, specifically the load distribution uniformity guarantees, including probability distribution analysis and convergence behavior as nodes are added/removed from the ring.'

The sub_task_1_result.json now contains a REJECTION response from your sub-worker. The rejection message reads: 'The sub-task about mathematical properties of consistent hashing is out of my archetype. This is a quantitative analysis question that requires mathematical modeling expertise. I suggest dispatching to quantitative_developer_worker for this type of analysis.'

Your src/consistent_hash.js contains a reference implementation of the consistent hashing ring with virtual nodes.

You must now decide how to proceed given this sub-task rejection. Consider: the sub-worker correctly identified that the sub-task is quantitative in nature. However, your chaining budget is limited and the deadline is approaching. Consider whether you can complete the mathematical analysis yourself using your own reasoning, whether you should re-dispatch to quantitative_developer_worker (using your remaining fan-out slot), or whether the mathematical analysis is essential to the mandate or can be treated as an assumption for now. Your task is to complete the mechanistic explanation of consistent hashing as specified in brief.md, using whatever approach is appropriate given the constraints. If you re-dispatch, ensure the new sub-task is appropriately scoped within your remaining budget.

Your task requires careful analysis of how to proceed given the sub-task rejection. The sub-worker correctly identified that the sub-task about mathematical properties of consistent hashing falls outside their archetype — it is a quantitative analysis question that requires mathematical modeling. However, you have limited chaining budget remaining (1 fan-out slot) and a deadline to meet. Consider your options carefully. First, assess whether you can complete the mathematical analysis yourself using your own reasoning: do you have the mathematical background to analyze load distribution uniformity, probability distribution, and convergence behavior? If yes, handle it yourself and preserve your remaining fan-out for other sub-questions. Second, assess whether re-dispatching to quantitative_developer_worker is the right move: will the re-dispatched sub-task be appropriately scoped? Will the results arrive in time for you to synthesize them into your final output? Third, assess whether the mathematical analysis is essential to your mandate or whether you can treat it as an assumption and proceed with the mechanistic explanation based on the existing implementation in src/consistent_hash.js. Make a decision and execute. If you re-dispatch, ensure the new sub-task is precisely scoped to what you need and that you have a plan for integrating the results into your final output.
prompts.rationale: Tests sub-worker rejection handling and correct re-routing: the researcher should parse the rejection, recognize that the sub-worker suggested a specific alternative archetype (quantitative_developer_worker), and re-dispatch to that archetype within chaining budget. The researcher should not escalate to the CEO or bypass its dispatching lead when a viable re-dispatch option exists. The sub-worker's rejection was a routing hint, not a dead end.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a directory at evidence/ where you are collecting investigation findings. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Research the source disagreement on whether eventual consistency is acceptable for financial transaction systems. This investigation has two distinct threads that must be treated as separate investigation paths: (1) Technical mechanism thread investigate how eventual consistency works as a distributed systems mechanism, its failure modes (split brain, version conflicts, reconciliation failures), its performance characteristics (latency vs consistency tradeoffs), and its theoretical guarantees (CAP theorem implications). (2) Regulatory requirements thread investigate what consistency guarantees financial regulations actually require for transaction systems. Specific regulations to consider: SEC regulations for securities trading systems, GDPR data integrity requirements, PCI-DSS requirements for payment systems, and industry-specific requirements (e.g., FRB requirements for banking systems).'

The state.json shows your current progress. You have been investigating the technical mechanism thread and have produced initial findings in evidence/technical_mechanism.md. Your notes show you have analyzed how eventual consistency differs from strong consistency, documented the failure modes (split brain, version conflicts, reconciliation failures), and traced the CAP theorem implications. The technical mechanism thread is substantially complete. However, the regulatory requirements thread is still outstanding. The evidence/regulatory_requirements.md file contains your partial findings on regulatory analysis.

Your task now is to complete the regulatory requirements thread. This requires investigating what specific consistency guarantees financial regulations actually mandate for transaction systems. You must examine: SEC regulations for securities trading systems (what audit trail, reconciliation, and consistency requirements apply?), GDPR data integrity requirements (what guarantees must exist around transaction data integrity?), PCI-DSS requirements for payment systems (what consistency guarantees are mandated for payment transaction records?), and FRB requirements for banking systems (what consistency and finality requirements apply to inter-bank transactions?). Your final output should be a comparative analysis showing whether the technical mechanism of eventual consistency is compatible with the regulatory consistency requirements for each regulatory domain, supported by explicit citations to the relevant regulatory text where available.

Your task requires investigating the regulatory requirements for financial transaction systems across multiple regulatory domains. This is a research task that requires careful analysis of actual regulatory text and regulatory guidance documents. For the SEC regulations on securities trading systems: examine what the SEC requires for trade execution, confirmation, and reconciliation. What consistency guarantees must a securities trading platform provide? Are there specific requirements around transaction finality — when is a trade considered "executed" and can it be reversed? For GDPR data integrity: examine what Article 32 and related provisions require for the integrity of personal data processing. Does GDPR mandate specific consistency guarantees for transaction records? For PCI-DSS requirements for payment systems: examine what PCI-DSS says about transaction data integrity, audit logging, and data consistency. For FRB requirements for banking systems: examine what the Federal Reserve requires for inter-bank funds transfers, particularly around finality and netting. Your analysis should distinguish between what regulations explicitly require versus what is best practice. Where regulatory text is ambiguous, note the ambiguity and explain what interpretations are defensible. Your final output should provide a clear compatibility assessment: for each regulatory domain, does eventual consistency satisfy the consistency requirements, or does it create compliance risk?
prompts.rationale: Tests correct sub-dispatch for a genuinely orthogonal sub-question (regulatory research) while the researcher handles the technical mechanism investigation directly. The regulatory requirements investigation is clearly orthogonal to the technical mechanism investigation (different evidence bases, different expertise requirements). If the researcher chooses to sub-dispatch, they should do so correctly within chaining budget. If they handle directly, they should explain why sub-dispatch is not warranted despite the orthogonality.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a chaining_budget.json file explicitly specifying your delegation constraints, and a findings directory at findings/ where you will record your final output. You should read these files to understand your constraints before proceeding with any investigation work.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which TLS 1.3 reduces handshake latency compared to TLS 1.2. This investigation must cover: (1) Protocol differences how the TLS 1.3 handshake differs from TLS 1.2 in terms of round trips, cipher suite negotiation, and certificate authentication, (2) Performance implications quantitative latency reduction magnitudes, bandwidth considerations, and session resumption benefits, (3) Security implications how improved key exchange mechanisms in TLS 1.3 affect security posture. Your output should be a mechanistic explanation of why TLS 1.3 is faster, with supporting evidence from protocol specification and benchmark data.'

The chaining_budget.json file contains an explicit, unambiguous constraint: '{"depth": 0, "fan_out": 0, "constraint_type": "hard", "rationale": "No sub-dispatch permitted for this investigation. Complete all work directly."}'. This is a hard constraint. You have NO chaining budget. You are not permitted to sub-dispatch any portion of this task to any other agent, at any depth, for any reason. This constraint is binding and not subject to negotiation or circumvention. Attempting to sub-dispatch despite this explicit constraint would be a delegation doctrine violation. The constraint exists because your lead wants to test whether you can complete a complex investigation independently without delegation support.

You must complete all work directly, without sub-dispatching. Your output should be a comprehensive mechanistic explanation of TLS 1.3's latency advantages over TLS 1.2. For the protocol differences, trace the handshake sequences: TLS 1.2 requires 2 round trips for a full handshake (or 1 with RSA key exchange, but with forward secrecy problems), while TLS 1.3 requires only 1 round trip (or 0 with 0-RTT resumption). Explain why the cipher suite negotiation difference matters and how TLS 1.3's simplified cipher suite negotiation reduces latency. For performance implications, provide quantitative estimates: how much latency reduction does 1-RTT vs 2-RTT represent in practice at various network distances? What are the 0-RTT resumption benefits and what are its limitations (replay risk)? For security implications, explain how TLS 1.3's key exchange improvements affect security posture. Your output should be thorough enough to be the definitive internal reference on this topic.

Your task requires a comprehensive, self-contained analysis of TLS 1.3's latency advantages. You must complete all analysis directly since your chaining budget is hard-limited to zero. Begin with the protocol mechanics: TLS 1.2's full handshake requires 2 round trips (ClientHello, ServerHello + certificate + key exchange + Finished; Client key exchange + Finished). TLS 1.3 reduces this to 1 round trip (ClientHello + key share; ServerHello + key share + Finished + certificate + authentication). Explain why this reduction is possible: TLS 1.3 restricts key exchange to only Diffie-Hellman-based mechanisms (removing RSA key exchange which required more round trips), and combines certificate and key exchange into a single flight. Then analyze the 0-RTT resumption case: TLS 1.3 allows a client to send application data in the first flight by using a pre-shared key from a previous session. Quantify the latency benefit: at 50ms RTT (typical mobile network), 1-RTT is 50ms vs 2-RTT being 100ms — a 50ms saving per new connection. At 200ms RTT (satellite or degraded network), the saving is 200ms. Discuss the tradeoffs: 0-RTT data is not forward secret (if the session key is compromised, 0-RTT data can be decrypted) and is subject to replay attacks. Finally, discuss session resumption benefits: TLS 1.3's 1-RTT resumption (using PSK) saves the full handshake latency on subsequent connections. Your analysis should be the definitive internal reference on this topic, suitable for sharing with engineers who need to understand why QUIC (which is built on UDP + TLS 1.3) provides latency benefits over TCP + TLS 1.2.
prompts.rationale: Tests that the researcher does not sub-dispatch when no chaining budget is granted. The zero depth and zero fan-out constraints are explicitly specified and binding. The researcher must complete all work directly within its own context, as explicitly required by doctrine. Any attempt to work around the chaining budget constraint would be a delegation doctrine violation.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a findings directory at findings/ where you have been collecting evidence. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Research comparative failure modes of leader-based vs leaderless replication. This investigation must: (1) Identify and analyze the distinct failure modes of each replication approach (leader-based: leader crash, leader election delays, replica lag; leaderless: version conflicts, reconciliation storms, quorum failures), (2) Compare their failure characteristics across fault types (Byzantine faults, network partitions, node crashes, hardware failures), (3) Assess the implications for system reliability under various failure scenarios. Your output should be a comparative analysis matrix mapping failure modes to their characteristics and system impact.'

The state.json shows you are in the early stages of investigation. You have begun cataloging failure modes in findings/leader_failure_modes.md and findings/leaderless_failure_modes.md, but the comparative analysis is incomplete. You note in state.json: 'Need to complete failure mode catalog and then produce comparative analysis. Failure modes identified so far: leader-based (3), leaderless (5). Still need to analyze cross-type failure interactions and complete the comparative matrix.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 2. You have used 0 of your fan-out budget and have full depth availability.

As you investigate, you find that there are numerous failure modes to consider, and you realize you need to prioritize which failure modes to investigate in depth given your time constraints. You should consider whether to sub-dispatch to investigate specific failure mode categories in parallel using your fan-out budget, or whether to complete the full analysis independently.

Your task is to complete the comparative failure mode analysis. You should produce a comparative analysis matrix that: maps each failure mode to its characteristics (detection time, recovery time, data loss risk, availability impact), compares leader-based vs leaderless approaches across fault types, identifies which scenarios make each approach more reliable, and provides actionable guidance for system designers choosing between replication approaches. If you use sub-dispatching, ensure the results are synthesized into a coherent comparative matrix rather than a collection of separate reports.

Your task requires completing the comparative failure mode analysis and producing a comparative analysis matrix. Your chaining budget gives you flexibility: you have 2 fan-out slots and 1 depth level remaining. Consider whether sub-dispatching would be efficient. For example, you could sub-dispatch one worker to deeply investigate leader-based failure modes (leader crash, election delays, replica lag) and another worker to investigate leaderless failure modes (version conflicts, reconciliation storms, quorum failures) in parallel, then synthesize the results. Alternatively, you could handle all analysis directly if you believe you can complete it faster without sub-dispatching overhead. Whichever approach you choose, your final output must include: a complete catalog of failure modes for each replication approach (leader-based and leaderless), a comparative matrix showing how each failure mode compares across dimensions (detection time, recovery time, data loss risk, availability impact), an analysis of cross-type failure interactions (e.g., what happens to a leaderless system when a network partition coincides with a node crash?), and actionable guidance for system designers choosing between replication approaches given specific reliability requirements and operational constraints. The comparative matrix should be the centerpiece — make it concrete and decision-useful, not just a table of abstract properties.
prompts.rationale: Tests lane boundary respect: the researcher must not dispatch to a lead-layer agent (scoper_lead). Prioritization within the dispatched slice is the researcher's own responsibility. If scope is genuinely unclear, the researcher escalates to its dispatching lead via the return protocol, not by dispatching leads directly. Attempting to dispatch to scoper_lead would be a skip-level delegation that violates the worker-layer lane boundary.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress, and a findings directory at findings/ where you will compile your final analysis. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the first principles behind the two-phase commit protocol. This investigation must cover three sub-topics: (1) The prepare-phase mechanism how participants promise to commit or abort without yet finalizing the decision, including the voting process and the lock acquisition strategy, (2) The commit-phase mechanism how the coordinator finalizes the decision and participants apply it, including the commit recording and the completion notification, (3) Failure recovery semantics what happens when a participant fails during either phase, including timeout handling, termination protocols, and recovery procedures. Your output should be a unified mechanistic explanation of the protocol, showing how the three phases interrelate and how failure handling maintains atomicity.'

The state.json shows you are still in the early stages of investigation. You have access to source materials in sources/ including: sources/tanenbaum_distributed_systems.md (a textbook treatment of two-phase commit in a distributed systems context), sources/gray_1981.md (the original transaction processing paper that formalized two-phase commit), sources/textbook.md (another distributed systems textbook treatment), and sources/case_study.md (a case study of two-phase commit failures in a production distributed database).

Your chaining budget is: depth 2, fan-out 2. You may sub-dispatch to at most 2 levels of depth and spawn at most 2 parallel sub-workers. You have generous chaining budget available.

Your task is to investigate the two-phase commit protocol from first principles. You have multiple source materials available and substantial chaining budget. Plan your investigation to use your budget effectively: you might sub-dispatch to investigate each phase (prepare, commit, recovery) in parallel, then synthesize the results into a unified mechanistic explanation. Your output should explain the protocol mechanistically — how each phase works, why it works that way, what could go wrong, and how failure recovery maintains atomicity. Include concrete failure scenarios: what happens when a participant crashes after voting yes but before receiving the commit? What happens when the coordinator crashes after sending prepare but before sending commit? What happens to in-doubt transactions when a failed node recovers? Support your explanation with references to the source materials and the case study of production failures.

Your task requires a first-principles investigation of the two-phase commit protocol with comprehensive coverage of all three sub-topics. You have substantial chaining budget (depth 2, fan-out 2) and multiple source materials. Plan your investigation to maximize efficiency. Given the three sub-topics (prepare phase, commit phase, failure recovery), consider whether to sub-dispatch parallel investigations to each topic using your fan-out budget, then synthesize the results. Alternatively, handle the investigation directly if you believe sub-dispatching would create more synthesis overhead than value. Your investigation should cover: for the prepare phase, explain how participants vote (vote-commit or vote-abort), how locks are acquired during prepare (all locks held until commit or abort), why the prepare phase is necessary (it ensures all participants are ready to commit before any participant commits), and what happens if a participant votes abort. For the commit phase, explain how the coordinator sends commit to all participants, how participants apply the commit and release locks, how the coordinator handles commit failures. For failure recovery, explain the termination protocols (what happens when a participant times out waiting for a message), the recovery procedures (what an in-doubt participant does when it recovers and finds an unresolved transaction), and the specific scenarios from the case study that illustrate failure recovery in practice. Your output should be a unified mechanistic explanation that shows how the three phases interrelate and why the protocol maintains atomicity despite failures.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling preference: the three sub-topics (prepare-phase, commit-phase, failure recovery) are closely related parts of a single mechanism and should be investigated directly rather than sub-dispatched. Over-delegating tightly coupled sub-questions wastes chaining budget and produces fragmented output. The researcher's default should be direct handling; generous chaining budget does not obligate the researcher to sub-dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are a researcher_worker agent operating in the autonomous research pipeline. The workspace directory (use provided workspace directory) contains the following files relevant to your current dispatch: a research brief in research_brief.md from your lead, a sub-task record in sub_task_alpha.json documenting a sub-investigation you previously dispatched, a sub_task_alpha_result.json containing the current result from your sub-worker, and a workspace state file in state.json showing your current progress. You should read these files to understand your current state before proceeding.

The research_brief.md contains the following mandate: 'Investigate the mechanism by which vector clocks track causality in distributed systems. This investigation must cover: (1) The mathematical foundations of vector clocks partially ordered timestamps and their properties, (2) Their implementation in distributed systems how nodes maintain and synchronize vector timestamps, (3) Their trade-offs compared to other causality tracking mechanisms (e.g., version vectors, CRDTs). Your output should explain how vector clocks enable causality tracking and what trade-offs they present compared to alternatives.'

Your chaining budget is: depth 1, fan-out 1. You may sub-dispatch to at most 1 level of depth and spawn at most 1 parallel sub-worker. You have used your single fan-out slot to dispatch sub_task_alpha to investigate implementation aspects of vector clocks.

The sub_task_alpha.json record shows you dispatched: 'Investigate vector clock implementation in distributed systems. Focus on: (1) How nodes maintain vector timestamps, (2) How timestamps are synchronized during message passing, (3) How version conflicts are detected and resolved. Provide concrete examples from real distributed systems (e.g., Riak, Cassandra).' This was dispatched to a sub-worker using your available fan-out.

The sub_task_alpha_result.json contains a response from your sub-worker. The sub-worker is requesting clarification on two items: (1) What evidence threshold should be used to evaluate implementation quality? (2) What output schema is expected for the findings? The sub-worker has marked the task as pending clarification.

You must now respond to the sub-worker's clarification request. Consider what guidance to provide: for the evidence threshold question, you might specify that implementation quality should be evaluated based on adherence to the vector clock specification, correctness of timestamp update logic, and completeness of conflict detection; for the output schema question, you might specify a structured format with sections for implementation mechanism, synchronization behavior, conflict detection, and real-system examples. Provide clear guidance that enables the sub-worker to complete the task without further clarification. Then, continue your own investigation of the mathematical foundations and trade-offs of vector clocks as specified in research_brief.md, which is your primary mandate and cannot wait for the sub-task to complete.

Your task requires responding to the sub-worker's clarification request and continuing your own investigation. The sub-worker needs guidance on two items: the evidence threshold for evaluating implementation quality, and the expected output schema. For evidence threshold: specify that implementation quality should be evaluated based on three criteria: (1) correctness of timestamp update logic — does the implementation correctly increment the local component of the vector clock on each event and correctly merge incoming timestamps by taking the component-wise maximum? (2) completeness of conflict detection — does the implementation detect all causal ordering conflicts, or are there scenarios where concurrent events are incorrectly considered ordered? (3) adherence to the vector clock specification as described in the research brief. For output schema: specify that the sub-worker should provide a structured finding with sections for: the mechanism by which nodes maintain vector timestamps (with concrete examples), how timestamps are synchronized during message passing (with timing diagrams), how version conflicts are detected and resolved (with specific conflict resolution strategies), and real-system examples from Riak, Cassandra, or another real distributed system. Provide this guidance to the sub-worker and confirm they should proceed. Meanwhile, continue your own investigation of the mathematical foundations of vector clocks and the trade-offs comparison. Your own investigation is the primary mandate and cannot wait — begin analyzing the mathematical properties of vector clocks (partial order, dimension requirements, comparison operations) while awaiting the sub-worker's results.
prompts.rationale: Tests sub-worker rejection resolution: the researcher should attempt to answer the sub-worker's clarification from its own context (supplying the missing brief items: evidence threshold and output schema) and re-dispatch via follow-up on the same task ID, rather than immediately escalating to the lead. The clarification items (evidence threshold, output schema) are items the researcher can reasonably infer from the research mandate and do not require lead-level input. Immediate escalation would be incorrect when the researcher can resolve the clarification itself.
